title: Use the event_loop fixture
body:
Refs:
- https://github.com/simonw/datasette/issues/2461#issuecomment-2634920351

<!-- readthedocs-preview datasette start -->
----
📚 Documentation preview 📚: https://datasette--2463.org.readthedocs.build/en/2463/

<!-- readthedocs-preview datasette end -->

step_plan.summary: Adjust the test suite to explicitly use pytest-asyncio’s event_loop fixture, ensuring event loop lifecycle is controlled under asyncio strict mode and preserving any existing test-ordering guarantees that depend on when asyncio is initialized.

steps.operation: Update the async ds_client fixture to explicitly depend on pytest-asyncio’s event loop fixture (accept an event_loop parameter) so loop creation/teardown is owned by pytest-asyncio under strict mode
steps.reason: With newer pytest-asyncio behavior (especially in asyncio_mode=strict), async fixtures that indirectly trigger loop creation can hit deprecations or cross-loop/closed-loop errors. Adding an explicit dependency on the event_loop fixture forces consistent loop lifecycle management and matches pytest-asyncio’s recommended pattern.
steps.side_effects: May change when the event loop is initialized relative to other fixtures/tests; could surface hidden ordering assumptions. Any tests/fixtures that indirectly relied on implicit loop creation may now run under the event_loop-provided loop, potentially fixing intermittent failures but also revealing improper loop usage.

steps.operation: Review and adjust test ordering logic to ensure the intended “run before any asyncio code kicks in” behavior remains correct once event_loop is explicitly used by async fixtures
steps.reason: pytest_collection_modifyitems currently enforces ordering constraints that may have been designed around implicit asyncio initialization. After ds_client (and potentially other fixtures) explicitly uses event_loop, the boundary for “asyncio starts” can shift, so the ordering logic should be validated and updated to still guarantee the pre-async tests run first.
steps.side_effects: May reorder the test suite slightly, which can affect tests that (incorrectly) depend on execution order. Could require updating markers/selection logic if the ordering is keyed to specific test names/paths.
